dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Breathing problem call at [CITY].
Hello sir, I'm Alex with [CITY] Rescue. What brought you to call 911?
I can't breathe and have severe swelling in both legs. It started two days ago.
Can you tell me your name, sir?
David.
What city are we in today, David?
[CITY].
What is the year?
Can you explain what's happening to you right now?
I am having difficulty breathing and my legs are really swollen.
Sir, we're going to take your blood pressure now.
Blood pressure is 161 over 128.
Pulse is 54 and regular.
Respirations are 21 per minute.
Oxygen saturation is 97%.
Can you describe when the swelling in your legs started?
About two days ago, and it's gotten worse. I couldn't sleep because of it.
Any chest tightness with the difficulty breathing?
Yes, it's been tight all day, making it hard to rest.
Have you been experiencing shortness of breath with this?
Yes, shortness of breath started with the swelling and chest tightness.
I see you mention COPD on your emergency medical information. Have you had any issues with COPD lately?
Yes, I was in the hospital for a week for an exacerbation a few weeks back.
May I check your breathing sounds, sir?
Go ahead.
Your breathing is a bit fast and sounds slightly diminished in the bottoms of your lungs. We're going to give you a bit of oxygen to help make it easier, okay?
Okay, that would help.
We are going to give you oxygen at 3 liters per minute now.
Oxygen saturation now shows 98%, no change to breathing rate.
On a 0 to 10 scale, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever felt, how bad is your leg swelling and chest tightness?
It's an 8, I don't want to live like this.
Can we roll you over slightly so we can look at the swelling in your legs?
Yeah, go ahead.
I see significant pitting edema in both lower extremities, more than +2. It seems painful.
We are going to take an EKG to monitor your heart activity. Is that okay?
Okay.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. We'll do a 12-lead for verification.
12-lead EKG is complete.
Partner, considering the signs and symptoms and prior COPD diagnosis, this is a respiratory distress evaluation. Let's follow protocol 3-11.
Heart rate has increased to 171.
Respirations are 22 now.
Blood pressure is 153 over 120.
Oxygen saturation now at 99%. No signs of worsening.
EKG still showing atrial fibrillation, no major changes.
Oxygen remains at 3L/min with no respiratory distress.
Anything changed with the pain since we started the oxygen?
It's the same, 8 out of 10. Doesn't hurt more, doesn't hurt less.
We are reading a fever of 103.3 F via tympanic measurement. Request permission to take blood sugar.
Blood glucose is normal.
We need to take you to [COMPANY_NAME]. Is that acceptable?
That's fine with me.
We will be securing you for transport. What is the position you're currently in for comfort?
I am sitting, that's the only position that feels good.
Understand, we will keep you in that seated position during transport.
Transport en route. Vital signs are stable for now.
[CITY] receive, we have a 60-year-old male with exacerbation of COPD, severe edema and fever en route, with vital signs stable. Arrival estimated in 14 minutes.
Any changes while on the way?
Stable, still breathing well, complains of chest tightness but no chest pain or shortness of breath increase.
Pressures remain consistent, we're documenting trends for the hospital.
Receiver, we have a clear line of roads. No complications with the transport itself.
All set. Vital sign readings are ready for follow-up reporting.

[CITY] receive, we have a 60-year-old Male with a past medical history of COPD, Atrial Fibrillation, and Primary Hypertension. Complaining of shortness of breath, severe pitting edema in both lower extremities, fever, chest tightness.
Arrived at 14:32, patient is alert and cooperative. Gave O2 at 3L/min, response improved and stable to this point.
Initial BP 161/128, HR 54, R 21, O2 97%. Latest at 15:00 with 153/120, SR 171, R 22, O2 99%. EKG is showing atrial fibrillation with rapid response and conduction delay.
Lung sounds are clear but slightly diminished at bases. No rales or wheezes. Temperature is 103.3 F. Swelling is severe bilaterally in lower limbs.
Patient is a&ox4/4 with good peripheral motor strength and sensation throughout.
Patient reported shortness of breath and difficulty performing daily functions for the last 2 days.
Severe fatigue and difficulty ambulating due to pain and swelling in both lower extremities were also reported.
We performed CV - ECG - 4 Lead Monitoring after arrival and prior to administration of oxygen.
Shortness of breath and dyspnea were reported by the patient as part of the breathing problem complaint from the start.
Blood glucose levels were tested and showed as normal during initial assessments and were normal in follow-up.
Blood pressure is 153 over 120, and we have consistent findings on recheck of vital signs.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness (signs and symptoms)
dialogue.utterance: You mentioned fever and fatigue. Tell me more about that.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness (signs and symptoms)
dialogue.utterance: Fever came suddenly 2 days ago. I feel very weak and haven't had the energy to do anything much at home.
Swallowing seems normal and speech is clear; no signs of difficulty, rhonchi, or strange sounds.
Abdomen is soft with no signs of tenderness or guarding at the moment.
He's well secured and comfortable for transport. No issues with airway or breathing during our last reassessment.
Noting the shortness of breath and soft bilateral lung sounds, we're keeping him in a seated position with no repositioning done en route.
He has periods of shortness of breath lasting about 10 minutes but has shown positive response to the O2 over time.
Along with COPD and heart-related conditions on his history, there is a concern for exacerbation, but oxygen saturation remains 97% and above.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history of present illness (pertinent past medical history)
dialogue.utterance: Just to clarify, have there been any recent changes to your medications or treatment for atrial fibrillation or COPD?

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history of present illness (pertinent past medical history)
dialogue.utterance: No, I've been taking Coreg, Flomax and Colace exactly as prescribed.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history of present illness (last oral intake)
dialogue.utterance: When was the last time you had something to eat or drink?

dialogue.micro_intent: retake history of present illness (last oral intake)
dialogue.utterance: I was up early for breakfast but didn't feel like finishing. Just tea and toast mostly.
Transport is smooth at this point; nothing to report in the last 5 minutes.
No signs of worsening breathing, and patient denies new chest discomfort or chest pain.
He denies any chest pain, nausea, or vomiting. Stated was on a nearly normal baseline with some spontaneous episodes.
The swelling appears to be in the lower extremities only. Further exploration is not conducted at this time.
Lungs clear but reduced in posterior fields with some chest tightness, which the patient mentions is persistent and uncomfortable.
There are no signs of chest wall pain or palpable masses. Pulses are strong and equal.
Patient reports good peripheral motor strength throughout. Mental status has not changed from the start and has stayed a&ox4/4.
A few sniffs as if working to clear nasal passages, but no significant rhinorrhea, nasal obstruction, or traumatic changes.
Based on initial and repeat assessments, we currently have no concerns for new cardiac events or pulmonary embolism.
Suspects incidental to prior COPD exacerbation. Suggests monitoring for possible secondary issues as fluid overload in the presence of leg swelling is a known concern.
We've had no new complications come up and given his stability, there's a low concern for immediate decompensation.
Patient also states no recent sick contacts or potential exposure to known COVID-19 cases, even though he had traveled from PA via bus four weeks ago.
Confirming [CITY] site. Case is managed as a COPD exacerbation with fevers and edema of recent origin.
No symptom changes, transport remains on time and stable.
